coredata.dc:title: Novel green synthesis of Value-Added graphene quantum dots from bagasse and pith for biological applications
coredata.dc:creator: Mohamed, K.M.
Paul Winston, Albin John P
Akash, K.
Sagayaraj, P.
Rajeshkumar, S.
Ravindhran, R.
Jayanthi, S. Amala
Vijaya, J. Judith
coredata.dc:description: The continuing rush for potential biomedical applications has sparked the fabrication of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) particularly from agricultural waste. GQDs are unique among carbon-based nanomaterials due to their flexibility, chemical stability, size control, and non-toxic nature. In this study, we have developed Graphene Quantum dots from sugarcane bagasse (GQDs-SB) and sugarcane bagasse pith (GQDs-SBP) by carbonization followed by microwave pyrolysis. The optical properties of the synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were characterized by Ultraviolet–Visible spectroscopy and Photoluminescence (PL) analysis and the surface functional groups were determined by FT- IR analysis. The High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was used to examine dimensions and structure of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP. The storage stability of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP was monitored using Ultraviolet–Visible spectroscopy. The synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were evaluated for biological applications like anti-oxidant activity, anti inflammatory activity and cytotoxic effect was also assessed. GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP demonstrated significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects due to their great ability to scavenge free radicals and extraordinary water solubility. The synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were less toxic in nature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Agricultural waste
Graphene quantum dots
Value-added products
Antioxidant activity
Anti-inflammatory activity
Cytotoxicity

Storage stability of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP
Cytotoxic effect of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Kothari Sugars and Chemicals Ltd for financial assistance under the project code: LCK23PHY001/002/CH001/PB001

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for the synthesis of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP.
(a) UV–Visible spectrum of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP; (b) PL spectra of GQDs-SB and (c) GQDs-SBP under different excitation wavelength.
FT-IR Analysis of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP.
HR-TEM images of (a–c) GQDs-SB and (d–f) GQDs-SBP.
Storage stability of (a) GQDs-SB and (b) GQDs-SBP at room temperature on UV–Visible absorption spectrum.
Anti-oxidant activity of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP.
Anti-inflammatory activity of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP.

head.ce:title: Novel green synthesis of Value-Added graphene quantum dots from bagasse and pith for biological applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The continuing rush for potential biomedical applications has sparked the fabrication of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) particularly from agricultural waste. GQDs are unique among carbon-based nanomaterials due to their flexibility, chemical stability, size control, and non-toxic nature. In this study, we have developed Graphene Quantum dots from sugarcane bagasse (GQDs-SB) and sugarcane bagasse pith (GQDs-SBP) by carbonization followed by microwave pyrolysis. The optical properties of the synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were characterized by Ultraviolet–Visible spectroscopy and Photoluminescence (PL) analysis and the surface functional groups were determined by FT- IR analysis. The High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was used to examine dimensions and structure of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP. The storage stability of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP was monitored using Ultraviolet–Visible spectroscopy. The synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were evaluated for biological applications like anti-oxidant activity, anti inflammatory activity and cytotoxic effect was also assessed. GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP demonstrated significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects due to their great ability to scavenge free radicals and extraordinary water solubility. The synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were less toxic in nature.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The emergence of a new biomass-based product is a uniting goal for the world in order to address conflicts caused by environmental pollution and the energy concerns (Chai et al., 2019). Based on the latest research, global abundance and environmentally favorable qualities of biomass render it a highly suitable substitute starting material for the synthesis of carbon-based materials (Ravichandran and Munusamy, 2022). Sugarcane bagasse serves as a highly efficient crop residue that is suitable for use in industry due to its vast output, consistent and stable attributes, inexpensive and centered manufacturing zone (Batista et al., 2019). Sugarcane bagasse offers a better prospective for use in the production of carbon-based nanomaterials due to its high content of carbon and oxygen elements as well as its low ash level contrasted with other agricultural wastes. But the usage of sugarcane bagasse, that is often burned outright for energy or utilized for creating bio-fertilizer (Alves et al., 2015; Arshad and Ahmed, 2016). Sugarcane bagasse pith is a short-fibred portion of sugarcane bagasse that retains an identical chemical structure but its use in paper production is restricted by its distinctive morphology and physical properties (Agarwal et al., 2023). Sustainable use of agricultural waste, such as sugarcane bagasse and sugarcane bagasse pith to produce carbon-based nanomaterials is beneficial in creating affordable products and assists in handling solid waste which contributes to solving pollution issues globally.

Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are a new class of carbon-based nanomaterial that was developed in 2008 and has gained the attention of researchers because of its intriguing properties (Sohal et al., 2023). GQDs stand out among carbon-based nanomaterials because of their features, which include water dispersion, dimension control, chemically stable, non-toxic, inertness, flexibility and excellent carrier transport ability (Chen et al., 2018; Zeng et al., 2018). These features were employed in a number of applications including Photocatalysis, drug delivery, light-emitting diode, fluorescence probing and analysis, bio-imaging and energy storage system (Gao et al., 2019). Different chemical methods such as electrochemical, hydrothermal, electron beam lithography and thermal treatment were employed for producing GQDs through carbon-based materials like graphene, carbon fibers, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), candle soot, coal and small particles like dichlorobenzene, glucose, etc (Pan et al., 2012; Peng et al., 2012; Shinde and Pillai, 2012; Wang et al., 2016; Ye et al., 2013). But many of these techniques require costly non-renewable raw materials like CNTs, carbon fibers, graphene, graphene oxide and its derivatives. These non-renewable starting materials needs to be treated in strong acidic condition at high pressure and temperature in order to break down into smaller GQDs, which restricts their large-scale production and also results in low yields (Abbas et al., 2018). The outcomes gathered from the above publications inspired us to utilize agro waste like sugarcane bagasse and sugarcane bagasse pith as the starting material for the synthesis of GQDs rather than those costly and environmental hazardous precursors.

Antioxidants and anti-inflammatory properties are often used in biomedical research as they contribute to the primary cause of the rise in diseases in biological system. DNA damage, protein deterioration, cancer and other illnesses are led by the formation of free radicals in the body (Gudimella et al., 2022). Furthermore, inflammation has turned into prevalent in modern life and has been connected to long-term diseases by damaging the structure of cell membranes. By taking conventional therapies often patients may lead to a number of adverse effects, such as cardiac arrest and kidney damage (Ashraf et al., 2022). As a result, it highlights the significance and demand of developing a substitute, like GQDs which have strong ability to scavenge free radicals, minimal toxicity and remarkable water solubility (Zhao et al., 2019).

In this research, we designed a more environment friendly, economical and scalable technique for synthesizing graphene quantum dots from sugarcane bagasse (GQDs-SB) and sugarcane bagasse pith (GQDs-SBP) by carbonization followed by microwave method. Additionally, we evaluated the antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties of the synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP. We have also assessed the cytotoxic nature of the synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP using brine shrimp lethality assay, which uses Artemia salina larvae. An inexpensive and effective framework is designed using an environment friendly method by sustainably transforms agro waste sugarcane bagasse and sugarcane bagasse pith into value added GQDs for biomedical applications.

Sugarcane bagasse and sugarcane bagasse pith were collected from the industry, Kothari sugars and Chemicals Ltd, SathamangalamVillage, Vettriyur Post, Ariyalur District, 621707, Tamil Nadu. Citric Acid was purchased at Sigma Aldrich.

Sugarcane bagasse was rinsed using distilled water for multiple times and then dried in a hot air oven at 80oC and the dried bagasse was crushed into a fine powder. The powdered bagasse was then burned in a muffle furnace at 600oC for 1h to obtain a carbonized bagasse.1g of carbonized bagasse and 1g of citric acid were dissolved into 50 mL of distilled water in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min. Then the solution was subjected to microwave treatment at 750Watt over 30 min. After that, the solution was filtered viaWhatmann filter paper No.1 and the filtered solution was dialyzed for 1 day to obtain GQDs-SB as shown in Fig. 1 . Same procedure was followed for sugarcane bagasse pith to synthesize GQDs-SBP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP
ce:section.ce:para: Several techniques were used to analyze the synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP including UV–Visible spectroscopy in which electronic transition of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were performed on HITACHI U-2900 Spectrophotometer. The specific photoluminescence setup aFlurologHORIBA was used to analyze the photoluminescence properties of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP in an aqueous solution. By exciting the GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP with a particular wavelength and monitoring the fluorescence that was released, the photoluminescence spectra were recorded. FTIR analysis (JASCO FTIR-4700, Japan) confirms the existence of functional groups in GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP in the wavelength region 4000–500 cm−1. The size, shape and structural characteristics of the synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were studied using aJEOL Japan,JEM-2100 Plus. The copper grid was coated with a drop of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP solution, and it was then dried over a hot plate and analyzed.

This study performed the hydroxyl radical scavenging assay to assess the antioxidant activity (M et al., 2023). The H2O2 radical scavenging activity was determined for the GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP. Different concentration (10 μg/mL-50 μg/mL) of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were filled to the test tubes and treated with 50 μL of 5 mM H2O2. Ascorbic acid was used as the standard. The blank control was made out of 50 μL of 5 mM H2O2 without any sample. The test tubes were left to incubate at room temperature over 20 min. The absorbance was recorded at 610 nm. The following equation (1) is used to calculate the % inhibition of H2O2 radical production.
(1)
%
of
inhibition =
Absorbance
of
control
−
Absorbance
of
test
sample
Absorbance
of
control
× 100

To perform Egg albumin denaturation assay, a mixture of 0.2 mL pure egg albumin and 2.8 mL phosphate buffer were prepared. Various concentrations (10–50 μg/mL) of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were incorporated into the reaction mixture. The pH was changed to 6.3. After that, it was left to incubate for 10 min at room temperature and kept in water bath at 55 °C over 30 min. Dimethyl sulphoxide was chosen as the control and Diclofenac sodium as the standard. Following it, the samples were analyzed at 660 nm using spectrophotometry (Singh et al., 2023).

Using the following formula (2), the % of denaturation of protein was calculated,
(2)
%
of
inhibition =
Absorbance
of
control
−
Absorbance
of
test
sample
Absorbance
of
control
× 100

The lethality test for brine shrimp was used to evaluate the cytotoxic potential of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP(Elumalai et al., 2023). 200 mL of distilled waterwas used to weigh and 2g of iodine-free salt were dissolved. Six well ELISA plates were filled with ten to 12 mL of saltwater. Ten nauplii were gradually added to each well (5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL, 40 μg/mL, 80 μg/mL and control). In addition to that the GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were then added in accordance with the amount of concentration. For a whole day, the plates were incubated. Following a 36 h period, the ELISA plates were analyzed to identify the quantity of live nauplii.

This was then determined using formula (3)
(3)
Number
of
live
nauplii
x
100
Number
of
dead
nauplii + number
of
live
nauplii

The UV–Visible spectrum of the obtained GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP is illustrated in Fig. 2 a. The absorption peak at 248 nm for GQDs-SB and 243 nm for GQDs-SBP corresponds to π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains and shoulder peak at 370 nm for both GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBPcorresponds to n-π*transition which is a characteristic peak for GQDs (Iravani and Varma, 2020). The luminescence characteristics of the synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were examined using the fluorescent spectrophotometer. As shown in Fig. 2b and c, the Photoluminescence spectra of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP at different excitation wavelength was analyzed. Both GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP show the excitation independent Photoluminescence behavior and had fluorescence peaks at roughly 450 nm and the emission intensity improved as the excitation wavelength increased from 250 nm to 340 nm. The highest emission intensity was observed at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm for both GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP (Nirala et al., 2017).
The surface functional groups of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were determined using the FT-IR technique. The FT-IR Analysis of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP is shown in Fig. 3 .

The absorbance peak of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP varied between 500 and 4000 cm−1based on FTIR measurements. The existence of the hydroxyl (O–H) functional groups is shown by broad absorption band at 3307 cm−1for GQDs-SBP as well as GQDs-SB and the existence of the CO functional group is shown by an intense band at 1640 cm−1 for both GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP(Kalluri et al., 2018).

The size and structures of the developed GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were examined and characterized using HR-TEM analysis as shown in Fig. 4 . The average size of GQDs-SB was approximately 7 nm and GQDs-SBP was 5 nm, respectively.

HR-TEM images highlighted the precise crystalline structures of the synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP (Fig. 4 c-f) with a distinct lattice spacing of about 0.24 nm for both GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP that was in line with the sp2 graphitic carbon diffraction planes (1120), suggesting that the crystalline state of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP was identical to that of graphene. Both the dimension and the lattice spacing of the GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP are in good alignment with the HR-TEM result that was obtained through different methods (Hao et al., 2015).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Storage stability of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP
ce:section.ce:para: The storage stability of synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP at room temperature was examined as a function of time. To check the storage stability, the UV Visible spectroscopic analysis for GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP was measured on the 1st day, 5th day, 10th day and 15th day as shown in Fig. 5 . There is no significant changes in absorbance peak of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP for 15 days which indicates the stability of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP during storage. The results indicate that the green synthesized GQDs from sugarcane bagasse and sugarcane bagasse pith could be stored for a considerable time at room temperature.

We performed the Hydrogen Peroxide Scavenging Assay to evaluate the synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP ability to scavenge the free radicals. The potential of the antioxidant to supply hydrogen ions is vital for the activity. To contrast the antioxidant performance of the GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP, ascorbic acid is used as the standard. The antioxidant activity was evaluated for variable concentrations of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP as shown in Fig. 6 ranging from 10 μg/mL to 50 μg/mL.

Antioxidant activity of GQDs-SB at different concentrations (10–50 μg/mL) showed inhibition at 49%, 52%, 64%, 75% and 86% respectively. For GQDs-SBP at various concentrations (10–50 μg/mL), the inhibition was 48%, 54%, 64%, 75% and 87% respectively. The inhibition was 90% for the standardand highest level of antioxidant activity of 86% and 87% for GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP was observed at 50 μg/mL concentration. The shift of hydrogen from GQDs surface to Hydrogen peroxide is associated with the process of radical scavenging. The presence of oxygenated carbon groups and hydroxyls (-OH) groups on the GQDs (which was confirmed by FT-IR analysis)facilitates the transfer of hydrogen atoms and degradation of H2O2. The findings presented above suggest that GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP displayed significant radical scavenging potential. Therefore, the synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP can be used as a potential antioxidant agent.

The findings of the present study were compared with the standard drug (Diclofenac sodium). The anti-inflammatory effect of the GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP (Fig. 7 )is in a dose dependent manner and findings were near close to the standard drug value.

The anti-inflammatory activity of GQDs-SB at concentration (10–50 μg/mL) was 54%, 63%, 66%, 71% and 78% respectively. In case of GQDs-SBP, the anti-inflammatory activity at concentration 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 μg/mL was 52%, 61%, 67%, 68% and 79%, respectively. Maximum anti-inflammatory activity of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP was observed in 50 μg/mL concentration which was 78% and 79% and for standard drug it was 81%. The synthesized GQDs from sugarcane bagasse and sugarcane bagasse pith shown to be effective as anti-inflammatory agents. Therefore, after appropriate and refined analysis, GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP can be utilized as a substitute for chemically produced anti-inflammatory drugs used for inflammatory conditions (Devi et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxic effect of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP
ce:section.ce:para:
A vital test to analyze Cytotoxicity is the brine shrimp lethality test, which offers details on the cytotoxic impact that bioactive substances possess on cells. As shown in Figs. 8 and 9
, Using various concentrations of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP, the viability of the nauplii was examined.

At different concentrations such as 5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL, 40 μg/mL and 80 μg/mL of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP, 100% of the nauplii remained alive after an entire day. Also 100% of the nauplii were found to be alive in the control. During 60 h, it was observed that at 40 μg/mL and 80 μg/mL concentration of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP, 90% of the nauplii were still alive. This finding indicates a clear correlation between lethality and concentration of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP. Due to their minimal toxicity, the synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP could turn out to be a more favorable and effective bio-agent in the biomedical area (Murugesan et al., 2018).

In the present study, an innovative approach to prepare graphene quantum dots using agricultural waste sugarcane bagasse and sugarcane bagasse pith was reported. The optical property of the prepared GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were analyzed by UV–Visible and Photoluminescence spectroscopy. The size and crystalline state of GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were determined by HR-TEM analysis. The synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP remained stable for 15 days at room temperature. The synthesized GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were tested for anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidant and cytotoxic effect. The results suggest that the GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP can act as potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant agent and GQDs-SB and GQDs-SBP were less toxic in nature. Thus, the present method provides a sustainable way to develop value-added graphene quantum dots using agricultural waste, sugarcane bagasse and sugarcane bagasse pith as a raw material.
This work was supported by Kothari Sugars and Chemicals Ltd for financial assistance under the project code: LCK23PHY001/002/CH001/PB001.

Mohamed K.M.: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Albin John P Paul Winston: Resources, Conceptualization. K. Akash: Methodology, Data curation. P. Sagayaraj: Resources, Conceptualization. S. Rajeshkumar: Methodology, Conceptualization. R. Ravindhran: Resources, Conceptualization. S. Amala Jayanthi: Resources, Methodology. J. Judith Vijaya: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Loyola College for the infrastructural facilities and support offered in completing the present work and Kothari Sugars and Chemicals Ltd for financial assistance.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile and cost-effective preparation of carbon quantum dots for Fe3+ ion and ascorbic acid detection in living cells based on the “on-off-on” fluorescence principle
Appl. Surf. Sci.
